mnemonics: SVD: Some Vectors Decompose - Remember that Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) breaks down a matrix into three special matrices: U, Σ, and V*
Eigen-VALUES Stay, Eigen-VECTORS Vanish - For a transformation, eigenvalues scale (stay on the same line) while eigenvectors give the direction (vanish along their own line).
keyPoints: Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) factorizes a matrix into U (left singular vectors), Σ (diagonal matrix of singular values), and V* (conjugate transpose of right singular vectors).
Principal Component Analysis (PCA) uses eigenvectors of the covariance matrix to find the directions of maximum variance in high-dimensional data.
The method of least squares finds the best-fit solution to an overdetermined system Ax = b by solving the normal equations A^T A x = A^T b.
commonMistakes: Confusing eigenvectors (from eigendecomposition) with singular vectors (from SVD); they are related but not the same, especially for non-square or non-symmetric matrices.
Forgetting to center the data (subtract the mean) before performing PCA, which is crucial for the covariance matrix calculation.
Attempting to solve the normal equations A^T A x = A^T b without checking if A has full column rank, which makes A^T A invertible.
